egypt.title: Goodbye Egypt, with respect and regrets
body: Egypt leave with pain, but also with pride.
They were very close to a historic win over Argentina, and led 2–0 before losing 3–2 in a cruel ending.
If this was Salah’s last great World Cup stage, he leaves as a true Egyptian football legend.

england.title: Bronze, brilliance and belief — England finish third after a ten-goal classic
body: England leave Miami with the bronze medal after one of the wildest matches this World Cup has produced. A 6–4 victory over France gives the Three Lions third place and a finish worthy of a generation that refused to let semi-final heartbreak define its tournament.

The first half was an English masterclass. Declan Rice struck early, Ezri Konsa doubled the lead and Bukayo Saka scored twice as England reached half-time 4–0 ahead. France were overwhelmed; England were ruthless, fast and fearless.

The match changed after the break. The introductions of Upamecano, Rabiot, Dembélé and Barcola transformed France, while Mbappé scored twice and the score tightened to 4–3. England bent under the pressure, but they did not break.

Saka completed his hat-trick from the penalty spot, Dembélé pulled France back once more, and then Jude Bellingham delivered the final word. His 98th-minute goal sealed a 6–4 classic and turned a night of chaos into a bronze-medal celebration.

Congratulations, England. This was not a consolation prize quietly collected; it was a medal won through courage, attacking quality and the nerve to survive a remarkable French comeback. The Three Lions finish third in the world — and they finish standing tall.
